name: Valleys of the Nacimiento and Andarax Rivers — circular from Alhabia
name_original: Valles de los Ríos de Nacimiento y Andarax — circular desde Alhabia
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Alhabia traverse a diverse landscape, from the fertile valleys of the Nacimiento and Andarax Rivers to arid plains and mountainous terrain. The region is situated at the confluence of two rivers, characterized by cultivated fields and vineyards, contrasting with surrounding arid lands. Access to the Sierra Nevada Natural Park provides opportunities for routes with significant elevation gain and varied vistas. Best road cycling routes around Alhabia The most popular road cycling route is Valleys of the Nacimiento and Andarax Rivers — circular from Alhabia, a difficult 37.8 miles (60.8 km) trail that takes 3 hours 8 minutes to complete. This route connects the fertile river valleys with varied terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Baños de Sierra Alhamilla and Andarax Valley Loop, a moderate 27.3 miles (43.9 km) path. This loop offers diverse scenery through the Andarax Valley. Local road cyclists also love the Gádor – Climb to Millares loop from Terque, a 16.0 miles (25.8 km) trail leading through varied terrain with a climb towards Millares, often completed in about 2 hours 9 minutes. Road cycling around Alhabia is defined by river valleys, arid plains, and mountain ranges. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to challenging climbs. The routes in Alhabia are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from 13 reviews. More than 400 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Alhabia's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Alhabia?
A: Road cycling around Alhabia offers a diverse landscape. You'll find routes traversing the fertile valleys of the Nacimiento and Andarax Rivers, contrasting with arid plains and more challenging mountainous terrain as you approach the Sierra Nevada Natural Park. This variety ensures options from gentle valley rides to significant climbs.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in the Alhabia area?
A: There are over 15 road cycling routes available around Alhabia, catering to various skill levels. These include moderate loops through valleys and more difficult ascents into the surrounding mountains.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: While many routes offer moderate challenges, there is at least one easy road cycling route available. The region's well-paved, often traffic-free roads make it generally accessible, but specific family-friendly routes might require checking individual tour details for elevation and distance.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e986048206/valleys-of-the-nacimiento-and-andarax-rivers-circular-from-alhabia" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valleys of the Nacimiento and Andarax Rivers — circular from Alhabia&lt;/a&gt; offer significant elevation gain over 60 kilometers. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39555941" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alicún-Huécija Viewpoint – Vícar to Felix Road loop from Terque&lt;/a&gt;, which features over 1400 meters of ascent.

Q: What is the best time of year for road biking in Alhabia?
A: Alhabia benefits from a warm, year-round climate, making it an excellent cycling destination even in winter. Average temperatures between 16 and 22 degrees Celsius in the colder months mean you can enjoy road biking comfortably throughout most of the year, avoiding the intense summer heat.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes near Alhabia?
A: Yes, many routes in the area are designed as loops. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984553171/banos-de-sierra-alhamilla-and-andarax-valley-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Baños de Sierra Alhamilla and Andarax Valley Loop&lt;/a&gt; offers diverse scenery through the Andarax Valley, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39554943" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gádor – Climb to Millares loop from Terque&lt;/a&gt; provides a moderate circular option with a climb.

Q: What kind of scenic views or natural features can I expect along the routes?
A: Road cycling routes around Alhabia offer varied scenic views. You'll pass through fertile river valleys with cultivated fields and vineyards, experience arid plains, and encounter mountainous vistas within the Sierra Nevada Natural Park. You might also catch glimpses of the unique landscapes of the Tabernas Desert. Notable natural features include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4525156" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Barranco del Cautivo&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6042540" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rambla de los Yesos&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any interesting landmarks or attractions to see while cycling?
A: Beyond the natural beauty, you can explore unique attractions. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/635124" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tabernas Oasis&lt;/a&gt; and the broader &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3988407" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tabernas Desert Natural Area&lt;/a&gt; offer distinct landscapes. You might also encounter viewpoints like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5302742" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Views of the Lawrence of Arabia Oasis&lt;/a&gt;, providing unique photo opportunities.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Alhabia?
A: The Alhabia area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from 13 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse terrain, ranging from river valleys to challenging climbs, and the well-maintained, often traffic-free roads that make for an enjoyable experience.

Q: Is there public transport available to access road cycling routes in Alhabia?
A: Alhabia is a small village, and while local bus services might connect it to larger towns in Almería, direct public transport options specifically for accessing trailheads with a road bike may be limited. It's advisable to check local transport schedules or consider driving to a starting point.

Q: Are there places to park near the road cycling routes in Alhabia?
A: As Alhabia is a village, you can typically find parking in or near the village center, which often serves as a convenient starting point for many routes. For specific trailheads outside the village, roadside parking might be available, but it's always good to check local signage.

Q: Can I find cafes or refreshment stops along the road cycling routes?
A: Many road cycling routes around Alhabia pass through or near other small villages in the Andarax Valley and surrounding areas. These villages often have local cafes or bars where you can stop for refreshments, especially on longer rides. Planning your route to include these stops is recommended.
tips: A few remains from the late Bronze Age (approx. 6th century BC) have been found in the municipality; Roman and Visigothic traces are lacking. It can be assumed that Berbers from the Maghreb were the actual founders of the town in the 8th century. The town was reconquered by the Christians at the end of the 15th century (reconquista), but was briefly given as a fief to the last Emir of Granada, Boabdil. Many Moors from the south of Spain retreated to the remote mountains of the Alpujarras during this period; isolated attempts at final Christianization failed, but after the Moorish uprisings of 1499 to 1501 and 1568 to 1571, the last Muslims were expelled or resettled at the beginning of the 17th century.

Viewpoint that is on the road that goes up to the town of Machal de Enix, from where you can see a panoramic view of the valley of the Río Andarax. To get there you have to make a few pans that take height. A lookout appears a few meters from the road, where you can ride your bike next to it.

In the Al-Andalus period, the town was in a different location. It was in a higher area called "lugarejo". Jorge Lirola assures us that Illar comes from the Arabic word al-Aliya, meaning "la Alta" (the high one).
